content.problemStatement:
Maheshmati and Sangu are playing a game. First, Maheshmati gives Sangu a sequence of NNN distinct integers a1,a2,…,aNa_1, a_2, \dots, a_Na1​,a2​,…,aN​ (not necessarily sorted) and an integer KKK. Sangu has to create all subsequences of this sequence with length KKK. For each subsequence, he has to write down the product of K−2K-2K−2 integers: all elements of this subsequence except the minimum and maximum element.

Sangu wins the game if he is able to write down all these numbers and tell Maheshmati their product (modulo 109+710^9+7109+7, since it can be very large). However, Sangu is a very lazy child and thus wants you to help him win this game. Compute the number Sangu should tell Maheshmati!
constraints: the numbers a1, a2, …, aN are pairwise distinct
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers N and K. The second line contains N space-separated integers a1, a2, …, aN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the product of all numbers written down by Sangu modulo 10^9 + 7.

sampleTestCases.explanation: There are four possible subsequences: [1,2,3], [1,3,4], [1,2,4], [2,3,4]. For each subsequence, we calculate the product of K-2 integers (excluding the minimum and maximum element). The products are 2, 3, 2, 3 respectively. The required product is 2*3*2*3 = 36.

There are ten possible subsequences: [1,2,3], [1,2,4], [1,2,5], [1,3,4], [1,3,5], [1,4,5], [2,3,4], [2,3,5], [2,4,5], [3,4,5]. For each subsequence, we calculate the product of K-2 integers (excluding the minimum and maximum element). The products are 2, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 3, 3, 4, 4 respectively. The required product is 2*2*2*3*3*4*3*3*4*4 = 162.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a brute force approach by generating all possible subsequences of length K from the given sequence of length N. For each subsequence, we calculate the product of K-2 integers (excluding the minimum and maximum element) and multiply it with the result of the previous subsequences. We use modular arithmetic to avoid overflow.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * (N choose K) * K log N) — where (N choose K) represents the number of possible subsequences of length K from a sequence of length N, and K log N represents the time complexity of calculating the product of K-2 integers.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — for storing the input sequence and the current subsequence.

code.Python3:
import sys
MOD = 1000000007

def powmod(a, b):
    res = 1
    while b > 0:
        if b & 1: res = (res * a) % MOD
        a = (a * a) % MOD
        b >>= 1
    return res

T = int(input())
for _ in range(T):
    N, K = map(int, input().split())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    ans = 1
    for mask in range(1 << N):
        if bin(mask).count('1') == K:
            sub = [a[i] for i in range(N) if (mask & (1 << i))]
            sub.sort()
            prod = 1
            for i in range(1, K - 1): prod = (prod * sub[i]) % MOD
            ans = (ans * prod) % MOD
    print(ans)
